ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for crayfish pasta; FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest broader class. Crayfish pasta is a pasta dish with seafood topping.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers pasta dishes broadly; no specific crayfish pasta code exists. Used as the closest defensible broader anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for crayfish pasta as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of crayfish pasta is approximately 300 g, comprising a plate of pasta topped with crayfish meat in a cream or tomato-based sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Crayfish Pasta — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Crayfish Pasta (cr_id F05060035). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for pasta with seafood cream sauce (USDA SR Legacy #21340) as a supplementary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (white, boiled) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crayfish pasta as a composite dish. GI of 49 is estimated using white pasta (boiled) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, which has a well-established GI of approximately 46–50 in the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of protein (crayfish, 5.6 g/100 g) and fat (6.43 g/100 g) further attenuates glycaemic response, supporting a low-GI classification. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (49 × 13.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 19.1 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content of 292 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 876 mg sodium, representing 38% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid adding extra seasoning or soy sauce to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content of 13 g per 100 g with a low GI of approximately 49; protein (5.6 g) and fat (6.43 g) slow gastric emptying and attenuate postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (150–200 g) of crayfish pasta, preferring whole-wheat pasta variants to increase dietary fibre and further reduce glycaemic impact, while avoiding cream-heavy preparations that add saturated fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (6.43 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat of approximately 2.1 g/100 g; sodium at 292 mg/100 g is a secondary concern for blood pressure management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, choose tomato-based rather than cream-based preparations to reduce saturated fat intake, limit portion to 200 g to manage sodium load, and pair with a vegetable side dish to increase potassium and antioxidant intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crayfish pasta is a Western-style pasta dish consisting of cooked pasta — typically spaghetti, linguine, or fettuccine — tossed with crayfish (freshwater crustacean) meat in either a cream-based or tomato-based sauce, often seasoned with garlic, onion, white wine, parsley, and olive oil. The dish is popular in Singapore and Malaysia as a café and restaurant staple, commonly served as a working lunch. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 132 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 6.43 g fat, and 13 g carbohydrate, with a low glycaemic index of approximately 49 due to the moderating effect of protein and fat on glucose absorption. Sodium content of 292 mg per 100 g warrants portion control for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains gluten (pasta) and crustaceans (crayfish), making it unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or shellfish allergy. Crayfish provides lean protein with some omega-3 fatty acids; tomato-based preparations offer lycopene and vitamin C. Cream-based variants increase saturated fat and caloric density. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crayfish pasta?
Is crayfish pasta high in sodium?
What is the GI of pasta with crayfish?
Is crayfish pasta suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060035) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous entry and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database pasta reference with documented analogous basis. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: Saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pasta with seafood cream sauce, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this item; this value should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if available. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient panels, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent crayfish pasta entry as of 2026-04-16. GI of 49 is an analogous estimate based on white pasta (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for crayfish pasta as a composite dish was found. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (49 × 13.0 × 3) / 100 = 19.1 g. The dish is not classified as Singaporean cuisine and therefore the multilingual naming requirement for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi does not apply; cuisine is Western/International. Halal status is unconfirmed as crayfish preparation and sauce ingredients may vary by establishment.
